champion.narrative: Hesham Taher (GM Procurement, Riyadh Cables) is the functional champion and sole active contact. He drove the 20 May discovery call, articulated the pain and requirements in detail, and led the 04 Jun demo. On 20 May, Hesham stated he would serve as project manager following board approval and that he and the VP of Supply Chain would assess options before presenting to the board. However, Hesham does not have access to the Economic Buyer or executive sponsor (no CFO, CPO, or CTO engagement), and the VP of Supply Chain (a key stakeholder Hesham named) did not join the 04 Jun demo and has never been engaged. Housam Kayali (VP Supply Chain & Business Planning, iContract Group parent) joined the 04 Jun demo unexpectedly but has not engaged since. Hesham's championship is now at risk: after the 04 Jun demo failed on the zero-touch PO requirement, he stated they would 'come back to us, but looking unlikely it will progress', and there has been zero contact for 27 days. The deal is single-threaded through Hesham, who is now passive/cooling, with no coach, no executive sponsor, and no multi-threading. The next move is to re-engage Hesham to assess whether the product gap can be resolved or reframed, and to secure an introduction to the VP of Supply Chain and the Economic Buyer (likely Baha Eissa, Group CFO) to prevent the deal from being quietly shelved.

competition.narrative: Competition is confirmed and reconciled across all sources. The primary competitor is Do-Nothing / Status Quo (remain with manual SAP + Excel process). Hesham stated on 20 May that Riyadh Cables previously evaluated SAP Ariba and Jaggaer but both attempts failed and were discontinued; they are now 'exploring all market options carefully' and have been 'very careful with their selection regarding scope, budget, and expected savings'. The Salesforce Competitors__c field lists GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER, and SAP Ariba as the broader competitive set, but no active rival vendor was named on either call. The real competition is inertia: after two failed vendor attempts (Ariba and Jaggaer), the buyer is risk-averse and may choose to stay with the manual process rather than take another implementation risk, especially after the Zycus demo failed on a hard requirement. The 20 May call noted 'Consensus view that Zycus offers a better interface, easier deployment, and more intuitive use than competitors', but that positioning advantage was lost on 04 Jun when Zycus could not meet the zero-touch PO auto-creation requirement. The wedge is to reframe the requirement (show that the two-click process delivers the same time savings and eliminates the Excel manual matching pain) or to commit the zero-touch capability to the product roadmap with a clear delivery date. Without that, the buyer will likely revert to Do-Nothing.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is explicitly confirmed and well-documented from both calls. Hesham owns the pain (GM Procurement) and articulated it in detail on 20 May: buyers manually review SAP PRs and issue individual RFQs one-by-one (unscalable with high PR volumes); contract-to-PR matching is done via Excel, resulting in missed contracted buying and redundant RFQs; no automated PO creation even when a contract exists; daily manual supplier follow-up by buyers on delivery dates (slow and error-prone); no spend visibility or analytics layer; slow sourcing cycles; margin pressure as a result. On the 04 Jun demo, Hesham restated the pain with specific examples: 'There is a need for an automated system to combine items into RFQs based on historical purchases and supplier categories, eliminating the inefficiency of manually processing each item individually' and 'The current process for supplier identification relies on manual checks using an Excel sheet to link items with contracts and purchase orders, which needs to be eliminated.' The pain is real, quantified in time (days to minutes/hours), and owned by the buyer. The issue is not the pain (which is severe and confirmed), but whether Zycus can solve it to the buyer's specification (zero-touch PO auto-creation).

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are explicitly confirmed from the 20 May discovery and 04 Jun demo calls. Hesham articulated five hard functional requirements: (1) Zero-touch PO auto-creation when a PR matches an existing contract (no buyer intervention, no clicks); (2) Auto-grouping of PRs by commodity/supplier into consolidated RFQs to reduce manual RFQ volume; (3) SAP S/4HANA bi-directional real-time sync (Zycus and SAP remain synchronized for PR/PO and approvals); (4) Automated supplier follow-up and delivery confirmation capture (eliminating daily manual buyer follow-ups); (5) Mixed PR handling (contracted and non-contracted items in one PR). On the 04 Jun demo, Hesham confirmed that the two-to-three-click PO process Zycus offers is insufficient and restated the requirement as 'the whole automated hands free process'. Implicitly, the buyer also values KSA local hosting and strong ERP integration credentials (they run SAP S/4HANA and prior attempts with Ariba and Jaggaer failed). The criteria are documented and confirmed; the issue is that Zycus cannot meet criterion (1), which caused the demo to fail.

However, the 20 May call shows clear AI interest: Hesham engaged positively with the Merlin intake demo (chat-based guided requests) and the Agentic AI autonomous negotiation agent. The call notes state 'The advanced AI capabilities, including generative AI and agentic AI for automated negotiations and guided intake, were showcased' and Hesham asked follow-up questions about the AI modules. The Salesforce Products field lists Merlin and ANA as in scope. This suggests the buyer is AI Curious (interested in AI as a differentiator and engaged with the AI demo, but AI is not the primary decision criterion). The deal did not stall on AI; it stalled on a functional workflow automation requirement (zero-touch PO creation). The AI appetite is real but secondary to the core procurement automation pain.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Customer expectations are explicitly documented and do not align with Zycus capabilities on the most critical requirement. Hesham articulated five hard functional requirements on 20 May and 04 Jun: (1) zero-touch PO auto-creation when PR matches existing contract (no buyer intervention, zero clicks); (2) auto-grouping PRs by commodity/supplier into consolidated RFQs; (3) SAP S/4HANA bi-directional real-time sync; (4) automated supplier follow-up and delivery confirmation; (5) mixed PR handling. Zycus is aligned on (2), (3), (4), and (5). Zycus is exposed on (1): the Zycus platform offers a two-to-three-click PO creation process; on the 04 Jun demo Hesham explicitly stated this is insufficient and confirmed the requirement is 'the whole automated hands free process' with zero clicks. This is a product gap, not a misalignment of priorities. The demo failed on this gap, and the deal is at risk of qualify-out unless the requirement can be reframed (show that two clicks still delivers the time savings and eliminates the Excel pain) or the zero-touch capability can be committed to the product roadmap with a delivery date.
